LensFlare Sample

The LensFlare sample shows how to use Xbox-specific features to create a convincing lens flare effect.

Overview

This sample uses both Xbox's visibility processing and its GPU texture copying abilities.

Path

Source: (XDK Root)\Samples\Xbox\Graphics\Lensflare

Remarks

The sample first renders a light sprite into the back buffer. It then uses the visibility of the light sprite as well as a "stretched" version of the back buffer alpha channel to achieve the light "flare out" and element rendering effects.

The algorithm for drawing the lens flare is as follows: Given a light sprite (alpha texture and color), a number of flare element sprites (an alpha texture, distance from light, and color per element), the light direction, and the eye vector, do the following.

  1. Clear the back buffer alpha at the light source (render the light sprite with the z buffer and color write disabled, and alpha write enabled). Set back buffer alpha = 0.
  2. Render the light sprite (with the z buffer enabled) to determine visibility and fill in the back buffer alpha. Set back buffer alpha = light texture alpha where the light is visible (passes z test). Set it to 0 where the light is not visible (fails z test). Set the visibility ratio = number of visible pixels / max number of visible pixels.
  3. Copy the portion of the back buffer rendered to by the light sprite to a texture. Transform the light sprite quad into screen space to find the source rectangle. Be sure to account for sampling artifacts and clipping of the sprite.
  4. Render the light in the "flare out" style. To do so, be sure to do the following.
    • Enable alpha blending.
    • Use the back buffer copy for the light sprite texture.
    • Render the light sprite n times while increasing the sprite's size on each pass.
    • Light intensity = dotproduct(eye vector, light direction).
    • Set sprite rgb = f( light intensity, light color.rgb ).
    • Set sprite alpha = f( light intensity, light color.a, back buffer alpha, n ).
  5. Render the flare element sprites along the element vector. To do so, set the following values.
    • element vector = ( screen space light position - screen space light center (usually screen center) )
    • distance ratio = length( element vector ) / max distance
    • position = element vector * element distance from light
    • scale = f( distance ratio )
    • sprite rgb = element color.rgb
    • sprite alpha = f( distance ratio, visibility ratio, element color.a, light intensity )

The sample encapsulates this algorithm in the CLensFlare class. It uses a structure to contain the light and an array of structs. This structure defines each flare element. The program uses the structure to create an instance of the class. After the parameters have been filled in, the sample simply calls CLensFlare::Render() while processing each frame to produce the desired results. You can tune the creation parameters to achieve a wide variety of lens flare effects.

The LensFlare sample implements the following optimizations:

  • The effect is culled if it is outside of the view frustum.
  • If the visibility ratio is 0, the effect is occluded.
  • IDirect3DDevice8::CopyRects is used for the back buffer copy.
  • Alpha textures are used for the light and elements.
  • The back buffer is not copied if the light is fully visible.

Further optimizations for an "in-game" implementation are possible. The sample stalls while it waits for the completion of the first rendering of the light sprite (it must wait for the sprite to be rendered to copy the back buffer and get the results of the visibility test). An in-game implementation could render other objects during this period to avoid the stall (as long as they could not occlude the light or render into the alpha channel of the color buffer). The light source could also be moved further overhead to avoid rendering the effect with other "expensive" objects near the ground plane. Finally, the code could be rewritten for speed, which may sacrifice clarity.

Unpublished work. © 2000 Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
